actions.thought: In Heads-Up No-Limit Hold'em, the Button (Small Blind) plays a very wide range of hands due to the positional advantage. K2s is a suited King, which is well above the median hand strength and certainly strong enough to open. Folding is too tight. While limping is an option, raising puts pressure on the Big Blind to defend and allows me to take the initiative. A minimum raise to 200 is a standard size at this stack depth (100BB) to risk the minimum while asserting positional strength.

I have Ace-King offsuit (Ac Kh) in the Big Blind, which is a premium starting hand. The Button (Pro) has opened with a 2x raise. In a heads-up match, AKo is significantly ahead of the Button's typical opening range. I will 3-bet for value and to gain initiative, as playing out of position is easier when you represent a stronger range. A 3.5x raise to 700 is a standard sizing to build the pot and force a decision from the opponent.

I opened K2s from the Button (SB), which is a standard steal attempt, but the Big Blind has 3-bet to 700 (3.5x my open). While I have position post-flop, K2s is at the bottom of my range and performs poorly against a 3-betting range. It suffers from domination issues (dominated by better Kings) and lacks playability compared to suited connectors or broadways. The price to call (500 to win 1400) requires about 36% equity, and realizing that equity with a weak kicker is difficult. Therefore, the disciplined play is to fold to the re-raise.

T8o is a standard hand to open from the Button in a heads-up or short-handed situation. I have the position advantage throughout the hand. Given the previous hand where I showed aggression and won the pot, maintaining a consistent opening range is important. I will raise to 300 (3x the big blind) to build the pot and potentially take down the blinds immediately.

I am in the Big Blind with A4o facing a 3x raise from the Button. In heads-up play, A4o is well ahead of a random hand and has sufficient equity to defend against a standard opening range. Folding here would be over-folding my blind. Calling 200 more into a pot of 400 offers me decent odds to see a flop, despite being out of position.

On a paired board of 5s-6d-6h, my Ace-high (A-4) actually constitutes decent showdown value in a heads-up scenario, effectively functioning as a pair of sixes with an Ace kicker. I beat all non-paired hands that don't have an Ace with a better kicker. Leading out (donk betting) would likely only fold out worse hands that I am currently beating and isolate me against better hands like pockets pairs or a 6. The standard and most profitable line is to check to the pre-flop aggressor to induce bluffs or evaluate a continuation bet.

On a paired, low board like 5s-6d-6h, the pre-flop aggressor typically maintains a range advantage with overpairs. Since Pro checked to me, I will place a standard 1/3 pot continuation bet of 200. This bet size is effective on static boards to fold out Pro's completely missed hands (like lower overcards) while keeping the pot manageable if I am called or raised. It allows me to take control of the hand and potentially win the pot immediately without needing to improve my Ten-high.

I am holding A4o on a paired board of 5s-6d-6h. Facing a small continuation bet of 200 into a pot of 600 (33% pot), I am getting excellent pot odds of 4:1, requiring only 20% equity to continue. In a heads-up scenario, Ace-high is often the best hand, beating all of my opponent's pure bluffs and non-paired broadway hands. Folding here would be too exploitable, and raising would likely isolate me against a range that beats me while folding out the bluffs I am currently ahead of. The most prudent play is to call and re-evaluate on the turn.

On the Turn, the board is [5s 6d 6h Qh]. The Queen is a card that connects better with the pre-flop aggressor's range (often broadway cards) than my typical Big Blind defense range. My hand strength remains Ace-high (effectively A-6-6-Q-5). Leading out (donk betting) here serves little purpose; it likely folds out the bluffs I am beating and gets called or raised by any Queen, Six, or pocket pair that beats me. The standard line is to check to the in-position aggressor. This allows me to control the pot size and potentially induce a check-back to realize equity or catch a bluff on the river. If the opponent checks back, I take my showdown value to the river. If they bet, I will have to evaluate based on sizing, but for now, checking is the clear optimal move.

The Queen of hearts is an excellent scare card that favors my perceived range as the pre-flop aggressor. Pro's check on the turn suggests they likely have a marginal made hand or a draw. By firing a second barrel (double barreling), I put significant pressure on hands like pocket pairs (77-JJ) or a Five. A bet of 600 into the 1000 pot looks like value from a Queen or a high overpair and is designed to fold out better hands that Pro might have called the flop with. Checking back would essentially concede the pot given my Ten-high has very little showdown value.

On the Turn, the board is [5s 6d 6h Qh]. The Queen is a significant card that favors the opponent's range (Button opening range contains many Qx hands) more than my Big Blind defense range. Facing a 60% pot bet (600 into 1000), my hand is merely a bluff-catcher. While Ace-high has some showdown value in heads-up play, the Queen turn makes it likely I am drawing to 3 outs or dead. I have many better hands in my range to defend with (any 6, any Q, pocket pairs 77+, and even some 5x). Calling here out of position with a weak Ace-high invites a difficult River decision in a bloated pot. The disciplined play is to fold and preserve chips.

I am on the Button with T8o, a marginal hand in heads-up play (approx. top 46%). Given the opponent demonstrated aggression by 3-betting my button raise in Hand #1, raising here risks getting 3-bet again and forcing a fold, which bleeds chips. Limping (calling) is a strategic adjustment to control the pot size, minimize losses against aggression, and leverage my position post-flop with a hand that has decent playability but isn't strong enough to withstand significant pre-flop pressure.

K9o is a solid hand to see a flop with in a limped pot. Since the SB just completed, I will check to see the flop for free rather than raising and building a pot out of position with a marginal holding.

I have King-high on a coordinated board of 8-6-4 with two hearts. Pro limped from the SB and I checked my option in the BB. Pro has checked the flop to me. With two overcards and no direct draw, I prefer to check back and take a free turn card. Betting here doesn't accomplish much as I have some showdown value against complete air, and I don't want to get check-raised off my equity on this wet board.

I hold Top Pair (Eights) with a Ten kicker on a board with straight and flush draws (8h, 6d, 4h). My opponent checked to me. Given the limped pot and the wet board texture, checking back gives a free card to too many hands that have equity against me (overcards, flush draws, gutshots). I need to bet for value and protection. A half-pot bet of 100 is standard to extract value from worse pairs (6x, 4x), draws, and perhaps A-high, while not being too large to fold out weaker holdings.

Pro limped pre-flop and I checked my option with K9o. On the 8-6-4 flop, I checked and Pro bet 100 into a 200 pot. While I have two overcards, this board texture heavily favors a limping range which contains many middle and low connectors. With no direct draws and facing an opponent who has shown aggression in previous hands, I will fold and wait for a more favorable situation rather than floating with King-high.

Nine-four offsuit is a very weak hand, even in heads-up play. While playing from the button typically allows for a wide opening range, 94o sits near the bottom and has poor playability post-flop. I have already established an aggressive presence in previous hands, and I prefer to maintain a disciplined range rather than overplaying junk hands. Folding here is a low-variance decision that preserves my stack and my table image.

I hold J4o on the Button (SB). This is in the bottom range of starting hands, even in a heads-up match. Although I get good pot odds to limp (need 25% equity, have raw equity around 47%), the playability of J4o is very poor. It has significant reverse implied odds and struggles to realize its equity, especially against an opponent who has shown aggression (3-betting in Hand 1). Folding is the prudent decision to avoid bleeding chips in marginal spots.

I have a pocket pair (4h 4d) on the button. In heads-up play, any pocket pair is a strong starting hand. I will raise to 3x the big blind (300 total) to take the initiative and build a pot, consistent with my previous aggressive play style in earlier hands.

I hold 86o in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise (300) from the Button. While I get 3:1 pot odds requiring ~25% equity, 86o plays poorly out of position and will struggle to realize that equity against an aggressive opponent who has shown willingness to barrel post-flop. Against a min-raise, a defense might be marginal, but against 3bb, this is a clear fold to preserve chips early in the match.

In Heads-Up play, hand ranges are much wider. Q6o has approximately 51.7% equity against a random hand, placing it well within the top 80% of hands typically played from the Button. While the opponent showed aggression with a 3-bet in Hand 1, folding Q6o on the Button is too tight and surrenders too much EV. Limping is an option, but raising to 200 (min-raise) takes the initiative, builds the pot in position, and gives the best chance to take down the pot pre-flop or navigate post-flop with positional advantage. If 3-bet, the hand is an easy fold.

I am holding Ace-Six suited (Ah 6h) in the Big Blind. The Button (Pro) has opened for a minimum raise to 200. In heads-up play, A6s is a strong hand that performs well both as a call and as a 3-bet. Since Pro folded to a 3-bet in Hand #1, I will put in a 3-bet here to 700 to take control of the hand and potentially win the pot immediately. Being out of position for the rest of the hand, I prefer to take the initiative or end the hand early.

I am facing a 3-bet to 700 after opening Q6o on the Button. As noted in my pre-flop plan, while Q6o is strong enough to open in heads-up play, it lacks the playability and equity to defend against a 3-bet. Even though I would have position post-flop, my hand is likely dominated by the opponent's value range (better Queens, pairs), and playing a bloated pot with a weak off-suit Queen is a losing long-term strategy. The pot odds (calling 500 to win 1400) require ~36% equity, which I likely do not realize well against a standard 3-bet range. Sticking to the initial plan: easy fold.

I am holding a strong pocket pair (9s 9d) on the button in a heads-up situation. Pocket nines are well ahead of an average random hand. My goal is to extract value and build a pot while maintaining control. I will open with a standard raise to 3x the big blind to 300 total.

I hold Ace-King offsuit (AKo), a premium starting hand. The opponent (Flash) has opened to 300 (3bb). Flash has shown aggression in previous hands (frequent opens and 3-bets). Against a 3bb open from the Button, AKo is a clear value 3-bet. Flat calling is possible to trap, but given the stack depth (90bb effective) and the opponent's sizing, 3-betting builds the pot immediately and charges weaker hands in his range. I will raise to 1100 (approx 3.6x his open) to extract value and mitigate the positional disadvantage.

I have pocket nines, which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. My opponent has 3-bet me for the first time in this match after folding to my previous 3-bets. While 4-betting is an option, I prefer to call here to keep the pot manageable and use my positional advantage post-flop. Calling allows me to keep their bluffs in and evaluate the board before committing more chips, especially since a 4-bet might only get action from hands that have me beat or are in a coin-flip situation.

I hold King-Ace (offsuit) on a Flop of 7d 7c Ad. This gives me Top Pair Top Kicker on a paired board. In a 3-bet pot, this board texture heavily favors my range (AA, AK, AQ). As the out-of-position player, I have a choice between c-betting and checking. Betting small (1/3 pot) is a standard option to extract value from flush draws and worse Aces. However, having the King of Diamonds blocks the nut flush draw, slightly reducing the number of draws he can continue with. Furthermore, the opponent 'Flash' has demonstrated an aggressive style in previous hands. If I bet, I likely fold out his air and underpairs (KK-88) immediately. By checking, I protect my checking range and give him an opportunity to bluff with his air or bet a worse Ace for value. Given the static nature of the board ('way ahead or way behind'), checking to induce action is a strong play. I plan to call if he bets.

I am in position with pocket nines on a 7-7-A board after calling a 3-bet pre-flop. The flop check from Pro is interesting. While it could be a trap with an Ace or a 7, it's more likely a sign of weakness or a medium pair like JJ, TT, or 88. My 99 has significant showdown value but is vulnerable to any Ace. By checking back, I control the pot size, realize my equity, and keep my opponent's bluffs in the hand. I don't want to turn my hand into a bluff by betting, as better hands (A-x) will never fold and worse hands might fold anyway. Checking back is the most balanced and safe play here.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (AK) on a board of 7d 7c Ad Th. After checking the flop to induce action and seeing the opponent check back, the turn brings a Ten, which completes a straight for QJ and improves AT/TT, though those holdings are less likely given the flop check. However, the opponent (Flash) has shown aggression in previous hands. Checking again serves two purposes: 1) Pot Control: It keeps the pot manageable in case I am beat by a disguised monster or two pair. 2) Inducing Bluffs: It signals further weakness, encouraging Flash to stab with air, semi-bluffs (flush draws), or value-bet worse Aces that might fold to a bet from me. If he bets, I plan to call. If he checks back, I will likely value bet the river.

Pro has checked for the second time on a board of 7-7-A-T. While my pocket nines are likely ahead of a significant portion of Pro's range that missed the flop, they are behind any Ace, any Seven, or a Ten. Since Pro 3-bet pre-flop, they could easily have an Ace. By checking back again, I am exercising pot control and looking to realize my showdown value. Betting doesn't accomplish much here, as I am unlikely to get called by worse or fold out better hands. Checking back allows me to see the river for free and decide based on the final board state.

The board is 7d 7c Ad Th 7s. My hand is Kd Ah. My best five-card hand is a Full House: 7-7-7-A-A. Opponent 'Flash' called a 3-bet pre-flop but has checked back both the flop and turn. This passive line strongly suggests he does not have a monster (like 7x for Quads, or AA/TT for a superior boat) nor a strong Ace, as he likely would have bet for value or protection on earlier streets. His range is likely capped at weak Aces (which chop with me) or pocket pairs (KK-88) which I beat. On this triple-paired board (Trip 7s), any Ace makes the same Full House (7-7-7-A-A), resulting in a chop. Therefore, checking serves little purpose as he will check back Ax (same result) and likely check back underpairs (I miss value). By betting small (~25-30% pot), I target hands like KK, QQ, or JJ that might hero-call thinking I am bluffing with missed high cards, while still chopping with any Ace. I lose only to the unlikely case 7. I will bet 600 to extract thin value.

On the river, the board is 7-7-A-T-7, giving me a full house (sevens full of nines). This hand is very strong, beating all three-of-a-kind hands such as KK, QQ, or JJ, which are likely components of the opponent's 3-betting range. I only lose to higher full houses made by an Ace or a Ten (e.g., AK, AQ, TT). Pro 3-bet pre-flop but then checked both the flop and turn; while this could be a trap with an Ace, it also frequently signifies a medium-strength hand or a missed high-card hand. Pro's river bet of 600 into a 2200 pot provides excellent pot odds of approximately 4.6:1. Given the strength of my full house and the favorable price, folding is not an option. Calling is the most prudent action to catch bluffs or thin value bets from hands like KK/QQ while minimizing losses to a trapped Ace.

I am on the Button with 5c 3c, a suited two-gapper. In Heads-Up play, the Button should open a wide range of hands (typically 80%+), and suited connectors/gappers are well within this playable range due to their post-flop potential and ability to crack big hands. Although the opponent has shown aggression by 3-betting my previous button opens (Hands 1 and 7), folding a suited hand this deep (100 BB effective) is too passive. I will open with a standard min-raise to 200 to assert initiative and utilize my positional advantage. If faced with a 3-bet, I can easily fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 9c3h. Pro (on the Button) raised to 200. Although the pot odds are attractive (100 to call into a pot of 300), 93o is an extremely weak hand that performs poorly out of position. Pro has shown aggression and solid hand selection in previous hands (like holding 99 in Hand #8). I will fold this junk hand and wait for a more favorable spot to engage.

Holding Ten-Seven of diamonds on the button, I have a solid suited hand that plays well in position. In a heads-up situation, opening for a raise is standard to apply pressure and build a pot with a hand that has decent post-flop equity. I have used a 3x raise size (300) in previous hands, so I will maintain consistency with that sizing.

I hold Jc 3h in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise (300) from the Button. J3 offsuit is a very weak hand with poor playability, making it difficult to realize equity out of position. Although the pot odds (requiring ~33% equity) are close to my raw equity against a wide range, the reverse implied odds and lack of playability against a larger-than-minimum raise justify a fold.

I hold Kh6h on the button (Small Blind). This is a solid starting hand in heads-up poker, possessing flush potential and high card strength. My opponent has shown a tendency to 3-bet my button min-raises frequently (Hand #1, #7), but also folded to one recently (Hand #9). Despite the risk of a 3-bet, raising is the standard play to build the pot in position and steal the blind. I will open with a standard min-raise to 200. If 3-bet, I will re-evaluate based on the sizing, but K6s has reasonable playability to potentially defend or fold cheaply.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Five offsuit. Pro has opened for 200 from the Button. The pot is offering me 3-to-1 odds to call (100 more to win a total pot of 400). While K5o is a relatively weak hand to play out of position, the price is right to defend against a standard button opening range. I have noticed Pro has folded to 3-bets recently, but a call here keeps the pot manageable and allows me to see a flop.

On a flop of 4d-8h-9c, I have King-high with no direct draws. As the defender from the Big Blind, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor to see their reaction. This board is fairly neutral but can hit various parts of a calling range. Leading here doesn't accomplish much with my specific hand, so I will check and evaluate based on Pro's action.

The board texture [4d 8h 9c] connects reasonably well with the Big Blind's defending range (containing many 9x, 8x, and straight draws). With King-high (Kh 6h), I have some showdown value and backdoor draws (flush and straight). Betting here offers little value as worse hands (Q-high, J-high) will fold, and better hands (pairs) will call or check-raise. Checking back allows me to realize my equity, control the size of the pot, and potentially improve on the turn without facing a check-raise.

The flop went check-check, which suggests Pro likely does not have a strong hand or is playing a polarized strategy. The 9d on the turn pairs the board, making it less likely for either of us to have improved significantly unless someone has a 9. My King-high has some marginal showdown value, and there is no reason to turn it into a bluff or bet for value here. I will check and evaluate based on the river or Pro's action.

I checked back the flop with King-high, and the turn pairs the board with a 9d. The board is now 4d 8h 9c 9d. My opponent checks again. My hand has showdown value against missed draws and lower high-cards. Betting here accomplishes little: worse hands (like Q-high or J-high) will simply fold, and better hands (any pair) will call. By checking, I control the pot size, avoid facing a check-raise, and get to see a free river where I might improve to a pair or win at showdown unimproved.

The board has paired on the turn and the river is a blank. The action has been checked through on both the flop and the turn, which suggests that neither Pro nor I have a strong hand. My King-high has a reasonable amount of showdown value in this specific line, as it beats all of Pro's missed straight draws (like 76, 65) and lower high-card hands. There is no merit in betting here, as I'm unlikely to fold out a better hand (like an Ace-high or a small pair) and I don't want to get raised off my marginal showdown value. Checking is the most prudent play to see if my King-high is good.

The action has checked through on the flop and turn, and the opponent has checked the river again on a board of 4d 8h 9c 9d 2s. My hand is King-high (Kh 6h). In a heads-up match where both players have shown passivity post-flop, King-high often has sufficient showdown value to win against missed draws (like 56, 67, TJ, QJ) or lower high cards. Betting achieves little because worse hands will likely fold, and better hands (like Ace-high or any pair) will likely call. Checking back is the lowest variance play to realize my equity and attempt to win the pot at showdown without risking more chips.

Holding 9s 7h on the button in a heads-up game is a standard opening hand. My opponent (Pro) has folded to several pre-flop raises and showed caution in previous hands. I will put in a standard 3x open to 300 to exert pressure and take advantage of my positional advantage.

I hold KQo in the Big Blind facing a 3x open (300) from the Button. In heads-up play, KQo is a premium hand that dominates a significant portion of the opponent's wide opening range. While a 3-bet is a viable option for value, calling is preferred here to keep the opponent's range wide (including dominated Kings and Queens) and to avoid bloating the pot while playing out of position. Calling also avoids the difficult spot of facing a 4-bet.

I hold Top Pair with a strong kicker (KQ) on a dry, rainbow board (K-3-2). As the pre-flop caller playing out of position, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. Leading out (donk betting) would likely cause the opponent to fold their bluffs and weaker hands, whereas checking induces continuation bets from a wider range (including air and weaker pairs). My hand is strong enough to check-call comfortably or potentially check-raise later, but for now, I want to keep the opponent's range wide.

On this dry, king-high board (3c-Kd-2s), a standard continuation bet is effective as the pre-flop aggressor. Pro's check is expected. By betting 200 into a 600 pot, I am applying pressure to his air hands and low pairs that likely missed this board. Since the board is disconnected, there aren't many draws for him to continue with unless he has a piece of the board or a strong pocket pair.

I hold Top Pair with a strong kicker (KQ) on a K-3-2 rainbow board. The opponent has made a standard continuation bet of 1/3 pot (200 into 600). On this dry texture, there are very few draws to protect against. Raising here would likely fold out the opponent's bluffs and isolate me against a range that crushes me (sets, two pair, AK). Calling is the best option to keep the opponent's bluffing range wide and extract value from worse Kings or underpairs on later streets. I am comfortable check-calling down.

I hold Top Pair with a strong kicker (KQ) on a board of K-3-2-T. As the out-of-position player who called pre-flop and on the flop, the standard line is to check to the aggressor. Leading out (donk betting) here would likely fold out the opponent's bluffs and air, which I want to keep in the hand to extract value. The Ten on the turn brings some straight potential (QJ), but it also might give the opponent a pair of Tens or a draw they want to bet. Checking allows the opponent to fire a second barrel with bluffs or value bet a worse King. I intend to check-call any reasonable bet.

The Ten of Hearts on the turn does not improve my Nine-high hand. Pro called my continuation bet on the flop, which indicates they likely have a piece of the board (like a small pair or a King) or a floating hand like Ace-high. By checking back here, I am controlling the pot size and taking a free card. If I bet and get called again, I'm committed to a very expensive bluff on the river with no showdown value. Checking allows me to see the river and decide my next move based on Pro's action.

The opponent checked back the turn on a K-3-2-T board, which caps their range and suggests they likely hold a marginal hand like a weak King, a Ten, a mid-pair (88-JJ), or air, rather than a strong monster (which would usually bet the turn for protection and value). The River is a 7, which is a brick. With Top Pair Good Kicker (KQ), I am almost certainly ahead. Checking here risks the opponent checking back a hand like Tx or a weak pair that has showdown value but won't bet. By betting a modest amount (40% pot), I can extract thin value from these second-best hands (Tx, 88-JJ) and ensure I get paid by any weak Kings that might have checked back the turn for pot control. The opponent has shown a willingness to call river bets in previous hands (Hand #8).

On the river, the 7d improves my hand to a pair of sevens. Pro leads for 400 into a 1000 pot after I checked back the turn, which is a common line for an opponent to take when they perceive weakness. While Pro could be value betting a King or a Ten, the price to call (400 to win 1400) provides me with excellent pot odds of 3.5:1. This call is profitable if I am right roughly 22% of the time. My pair of sevens now beats his potential low pocket pairs (44, 55, 66) that he might have been peeling with, as well as any air or high-card floats he is choosing to turn into a bluff. Given the board and the betting sequence, a call is the most appropriate decision for a mid-strength hand at this price.

I hold 2c 5h on the Button (SB), which is statistically one of the weakest starting hands in heads-up poker. My opponent (Flash) has shown aggression from the Big Blind, 3-betting my button raises in 2 out of 4 previous instances (Hand #1 and Hand #7). Attempting to steal the blinds with such a weak hand carries high risk against an opponent willing to re-raise. Limping is also unattractive as the hand has poor post-flop playability. Therefore, the standard and disciplined play is to fold.

I am on the button with Qc 3s. My stack is slightly smaller than my opponent's. Q3o is a marginal hand, but in a heads-up situation, it's generally worth a raise or at least a completion. Given that 'Pro' has been somewhat aggressive, I'll attempt to take control of the pot early by raising to 3x the big blind. This puts pressure on the big blind and allows me to win the pot immediately if they fold. If they call, I have position for the rest of the hand.

I hold 8s 4s in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise (300 chips) from the Button. While the pot odds are 33% (calling 200 into a total pot of 600), my raw equity against a standard button opening range is roughly 35-37%. However, being out of position with a disconnected, low suited hand makes realizing this equity difficult, especially against an opponent who sizes up pre-flop and is capable of applying post-flop pressure. The 'gap' between the cards and low rank creates reverse implied odds issues. Against a min-raise, a defense might be justifiable, but against a 3x open, folding is the prudent play to conserve chips.

I hold King-Five offsuit (K5o) on the Button. In heads-up play, holding a King is statistically strong, and this hand falls well within a standard opening range (typically top 80-90% of hands). Although the opponent (Flash) has shown aggression by 3-betting my button raises previously (Hand #1 and #7), I must maintain aggression to steal the blinds and utilize my positional advantage. Limping is an option to counteract the 3-bets, but raising to 200 puts immediate pressure on the Big Blind and prevents them from realizing equity for free. If I face a 3-bet, I can comfortably fold this specific hand without significant loss.

I am in the Big Blind holding Qc 3h, a very weak hand. Pro has raised to 200 from the Small Blind. Although I only need to call 100 more to see a flop, giving me 3:1 pot odds, Q3 offsuit is near the bottom of my range and performs poorly out of position. Given the tournament context and the need to preserve my stack against an aggressive opponent, I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with Td6s. In a heads-up game, 10-6 offsuit is a marginal hand but generally worth playing from the button to maintain pressure and exploit position. The current pot is 150 and it costs 50 to call. However, a standard raise is usually more effective here to take control of the hand. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and potentially win it pre-flop, or set up a continuation bet on the flop if called.

I am in the Big Blind with K9o facing a 3bb raise (300) from the Button (Flash). My opponent has been consistently opening to 300 on the button, suggesting a standard wide opening range. Pot odds are 200 to call into a pot of 400, offering me 3:1 (33% equity required). K9o has significantly more equity than 33% against a typical heads-up button range (often 50%+). While I will be out of position post-flop, the hand is too strong to fold. 3-betting is an option but risks isolating me against a stronger range. Calling is the most standard, positive expected value play to realize my equity.

I am out of position on a board of [Ts Jc Ad], which heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range as it connects well with broadway cards and Aces. I hold Kd9h, giving me a gutshot straight draw (needing a Queen) and King-high. Leading out (donk betting) is ill-advised on this texture because my opponent likely connects with this board and I have very little showdown value currently. The standard play is to check to the aggressor. If the opponent bets, I will evaluate based on the size, but for now, checking is the correct move to try to realize equity for free or keep the pot small.

I have third pair on a very coordinated board (A-J-T). While I have some showdown value with my pair of Tens, this board hits a significant portion of the opponent's pre-flop calling range (Aces, Jacks, and straight draws). Betting here is unlikely to fold out better hands and might induce a check-raise. By checking back, I control the pot size and realize my equity while potentially seeing a safe turn card.

The turn brings the 7d, putting two diamonds on the board [Ts Jc Ad 7d]. The opponent checked back the flop, which is a wet board that heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. I have trip Kings on a very dry board, and my opponent called a flop continuation bet, indicating they likely have a marginal piece of the board like a pair of sixes, a pocket pair, or a float with Ace-high. A bet of 600, which is half the size of the pot, is a standard value-betting size that remains consistent with my flop action and keeps Pro's weaker range in the hand.

I hold Ace-Jack offsuit (Ace-high) on a paired board of Kh-Kd-6c-2c. I defended the Big Blind and called a c-bet on the flop. The turn brings a low card and a club flush draw, and my opponent (Flash) fires a second barrel of 600 (half pot). While my hand beats pure bluffs (like straight draws or random air), the double barrel on this texture is polarizing. Flash represents a King, a pocket pair, or a strong draw. With no pair and no draw of my own, my equity is fragile; I am drawing to 3 outs against a pocket pair and am drawing dead against a King. Given I am the chip leader, there is no need to make a high-variance hero call for 20% of the pot equity without a stronger read or better holdings. Folding preserves my stack dominance for better spots.

I hold pocket threes (3s 3d) on the Button/Small Blind. In heads-up play, any pocket pair is a respectable hand, usually ahead of a random range. My stack is deep (58 BB effective), and I have position. Limping is an option to keep the pot small, but raising to 200 (min-raise) is standard to deny free equity to the Big Blind's random holdings (like J2o or 74o) which have live cards against me. If the opponent 3-bets, I can easily fold this small pair. If called, I will play in position with the potential to hit a set or win small pots on favorable boards. Given the opponent's tendency to defend or 3-bet, a min-raise is a controlled aggression play.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ten-Nine offsuit. The Button (Pro) has raised to 2x the big blind (200). I only need to call 100 more to see a flop in a pot of 400, giving me 4:1 odds. T9o is a strong enough hand to defend against a wide button opening range, especially given the excellent pot odds and the playability of the hand post-flop.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Nine offsuit on a K-4-3 rainbow/flush draw board. The board does not favor my hand significantly. I have two overcards to the 4 and 3, but I'm behind any King. I have flopped a set of threes (bottom set) on a board of Ks 4h 3h. This is an extremely strong hand. The board texture contains a King, which connects well with the Big Blind's defending range (Kx), and a flush draw (hearts). I need to bet for value and protection. Checking back risks giving a free card to draws and missing value from top pair. A bet of half the pot (200) is a standard size that balances getting value while keeping the opponent's range wide enough to call with worse hands.

On a King-high board where the pre-flop aggressor has a clear range advantage, my Ten-high has very little equity. I have no draws and no pairs. Calling a half-pot bet with no showdown value and poor prospects of improving is not a winning strategy, especially against a player with a significant chip lead. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with Ace-Five offsuit, which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play. I am in the Big Blind with Q9o against a Button raise to 300 (3bb). The opponent, Flash, has been raising this size frequently from the button. I hold Top Pair with a good kicker (Queens) on a paired board (9h 8c 8h). I plan to call or potentially check-raise if he bets, depending on the sizing.

I have Ace-high on a board of 9h-8c-8h. While I haven't hit a pair, my Ace-high has decent showdown value in a heads-up pot. I hold Top Pair (Nines) with a good kicker (Queen) on a paired board (9h 8c 8h). This is a strong hand in a heads-up scenario. Raising would likely fold out the bluffs I want to keep in and isolate me against trips (8x) or overpairs. Calling allows me to control the pot size and re-evaluate on the turn while keeping the opponent's range wide.

I hold Top Pair (Nines) with a Queen kicker on a board of [9h 8c 8h 4d]. The turn card is a brick that does not change the texture of the board or complete any immediate draws. My hand remains strong relative to the opponent's wide button range. I am out of position. The standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor who also bet the flop. Leading out (donking) would likely fold out the opponent's bluffs and only get action from hands that beat me (like trips or overpairs) or strong draws. By checking, I give the opponent the opportunity to fire a second barrel with semi-bluffs (flush/straight draws) or worse value hands, or to check back for pot control. I have Ace-high on a paired board. After my flop continuation bet was called, the turn 4d is a brick. My Ace-high still has some showdown value, and checking back allows me to control the size of the pot and see a free river. Betting again might not fold out a better hand like a 9 or a pocket pair, and it risks a check-raise from an 8 or a slow-played monster.

I hold Top Pair (Nines) with a strong Queen kicker on a paired board (9h 8c 8h 4d 6d). Checking is the superior play to induce bluffs from the opponent's missed draws (like hearts or JT) or to get to a cheap showdown against his marginal made hands. My hand is strong enough to call a reasonable bet (bluff-catch), but betting myself likely folds out the hands I want to keep in.

I have Ace-high on a board that paired early (8-8). After my flop continuation bet was called, the turn and river were checked by both players. My Ace-high has significant showdown value in a heads-up pot, as it beats all missed heart draws and straight draws (like J-T or Q-J). There is no reason to bet here, as a better hand like a 9 or a small pocket pair is unlikely to fold, and a worse hand is unlikely to call. Checking back ensures I reach showdown without further risk.

I hold Pocket Kings (Kc Kh), a premium starting hand. Being on the button/SB with a significant chip lead, my goal is to build the pot and extract value. I have flopped trips (nines) with a six kicker on a board of 9c-9d-Ad. The Pro opened pre-flop, which means their range is heavily weighted towards high cards, including many Aces. I hold Pocket Kings on a board of 9c-9d-Ad. The Ace is a significant scare card. While the Ace hits my perceived range as the pre-flop raiser, it downgrades my actual hand strength significantly. I have flopped trips and turned a full house (nines full of sixes). I hold Pocket Kings (Kc Kh). The board is [9c 9d Ad 6d]. I checked back the flop, controlling the pot on an Ace-high board. The turn brings a third diamond, completing a possible flush. The combination of the Ace, the paired board, and the completed flush makes it highly probable I am behind, and I have few to no outs if I am. As the chip leader, I should avoid bleeding chips in spots where my range is capped and the board heavily favors the opponent's range. A disciplined fold is the correct play.

I am on the button with Q2o. While it is a weak hand, in heads-up play, maintaining aggression from the button is important, especially since I am the shorter stack and need to pick up pots. I am in the Small Blind/Button with Q9o, which is an above-average hand in heads-up play. I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Ten offsuit (ATo), which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. My opponent, Pro, has a significant stack lead and has been active with 2x open-raises. I hold Q9o on the Button/SB and raised to 200, but Flash 3-bet to 700 from the BB. This is a large 3-bet (3.5x my open). Given my significant chip lead (14.4k vs 4.7k), I can afford to wait for a better spot or a hand with better post-flop playability (like suited connectors or Ax) to defend. Folding minimizes variance and avoids a marginal negative EV situation.

I am on the button with JT offsuit, which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play. I am in the Big Blind with [5h 8d], a very weak offsuit hand. My opponent, Flash, has raised to 300 (3BB) from the Button. I hold 4c 2s in the Small Blind. This is 4-2 offsuit, one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. In the Small Blind with T5o, I have a relatively weak hand. My opponent (Pro) has been playing somewhat cautiously but has shown they can defend their blind or fold to aggression. I hold King-Four suited (K4s) on the button, which is a standard opening hand in heads-up play due to its flush potential and high card value. I am in the Big Blind with 8c 3d. The Pro player has raised to 200 from the Button/Small Blind. To call, I only need to put in 100 more chips into a pot that will be 400. I have been dealt Pocket Nines on the button. This is a premium hand in a heads-up situation. I hold 9s3h in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise (300 total) from the opponent on the Button. I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Four offsuit. Pro has opened for a minimum raise to 200. While the pot odds to call are 3:1, this hand is extremely weak and plays poorly out of position. I am in the Big Blind with Q2o facing a 3x raise (300 chips) from the Button (Flash). Q2o is a weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position. I am holding Ace-Four offsuit, which is a strong starting hand in a heads-up match. I am on the button with Queen-Six offsuit. In heads-up play, this is a reasonable hand to open with to put pressure on the Big Blind. I am in the Big Blind with Q7o facing a 3x raise (300) from the Button (Flash). I am in the Small Blind (Button) with T8o. In Heads-Up play, this hand is within the standard opening range. I have AQo in the Big Blind. My opponent, Pro, has been opening frequently and I have a strong hand that dominates a large portion of their opening range. I opened to 200 with T8o, and the opponent (Flash) 3-bet to 700. The pot is 900, and I must call 500 to see a flop, offering pot odds of roughly 1.8:1 (requiring ~36% equity). I am on the button with Jd 8s. In a heads-up situation, J8o is a standard opening hand. I hold Kh 4d in the Big Blind facing a 3BB open raise (300 chips) from the Button. I am on the Button with J6o. Looking at the history of previous hands where the opponent (Pro) was the hero, they have shown an extremely tight folding range. I am in the Big Blind with T2o, facing a 3x raise (300) from the Button. My hand is extremely weak (bottom 15%) and performs poorly post-flop, especially out of position. I hold Pocket Nines (9c 9s), which is a premium starting hand in heads-up play. From the Button (Small Blind), I want to build the pot for value. With 85 offsuit in the Big Blind, I am getting 3:1 odds to call the 100-chip raise. While my hand is weak, Pro's opening range is wide in heads-up play, and I have a significant stack to defend. Calling allows me to see a flop cheaply and potentially outplay him post-flop or hit a strong draw.

I am out of position on an Ace-high board after calling a pre-flop raise. This board structure heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range. With 8-high and very little equity or draw potential, leading out into the aggressor is not advisable. I will check to see how the opponent reacts, likely planning to fold to a significant bet.

I hold a pair of Nines on an A-6-2 board. The presence of the Ace makes my hand a second pair, which is a marginal showdown value hand. This is a classic 'way ahead or way behind' situation; either the opponent has an Ace and I am behind, or they have a lower pair/nothing and I am ahead. Betting here folds out worse hands that I am already beating (like King-high) while keeping in better hands (Ax) and strong draws. By checking back, I control the pot size, disguise my hand strength, and potentially induce bluffs from the opponent on later streets. I avoid being check-raised, which would force me to fold a hand with decent showdown value. Therefore, checking is the most prudent play.

I am in the Big Blind with 8s 5d on a board of 6d 2d Ah Td. I have a low diamond (5d) which gives me a flush draw. On the flop, both I and the opponent checked, which suggests the opponent might not have a strong Ace or they are playing cautiously. I hope to see a free river card or will evaluate if the opponent bets small, though I am likely folding to significant pressure given the strength of an Ace-high board for the pre-flop raiser's range.

On the Turn, the board is [6d 2d Ah Td]. The Ten of Diamonds puts three diamonds on the board and is an overcard to my pair of Nines. By checking, I control the size of the pot, avoid being blown off my equity by a check-raise, and try to get to a cheap showdown to see if my hand is good against missed draws or smaller pairs.

I have completed a 5-high flush on the river (5d 8s on a 6d 2d Ah Td 9d board). On a four-diamond board, any diamond in the opponent's hand that is higher than the 5 (7d, 8d, Jd, Qd, Kd) will win. Checking allows me to reach showdown for free or catch a potential bluff from a non-diamond hand.

The board has run out [6d 2d Ah Td 9d], putting four diamonds on the board. I hold a set of Nines, which is a strong hand in terms of absolute rank, but vulnerable on this specific texture. On the river, the board reads 7s 2c 7d 9h 6d. I hold Ace-Jack (Ad Js), giving me Ace-high. On the river, the six of diamonds completes my gutshot straight (5-6-7-8-9). I am on the button with Jd 8c. In heads-up play, this is a standard opening hand. My opponent has shown a tendency to fold to pre-flop pressure or 3-bet aggressively. I will stick to my standard open of 3x the big blind to 300 to take control of the pot early.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Ten offsuit (KTo), a strong hand in heads-up play that plays well against a wide button opening range. I hold Top Pair with a strong King kicker on a dynamic board (Th 4c 5c). My opponent has c-bet half the pot (300). I hold Top Pair (Tens) with a King kicker on a board of [Th 4c 5c 9c 9h]. The turn brought a third club, completing potential flushes, but my opponent checked back. On the river, the board is [Th 4c 5c 9c 9h]. I hold Top Pair (Tens) with a King kicker. The key to this hand is the opponent's check-back on the turn (9c). The turn card completed the flush draw. I hold Trip Queens with a King kicker (Ks Qd) on a [Qs Qh 6s 7h 3c] board. The opponent 3-bet pre-flop and barreled the flop and turn before checking the river. The pot is substantial at 4600. Raising here isolates me against flushes and folds out the bluffs or weaker hands (like a Ten or a smaller pair) that I want to keep in the pot. By calling, I keep his range wide, control the size of the pot in case he does have the flush, and retain position to extract value or induce bluffs on the River. I hold Trip Eights (8c 8h 8d) with a King kicker, which is a strong hand in isolation. However, the board runout [3d 8d 8h Td 9d] is incredibly dangerous, putting four diamonds on the table. On four-flush boards, bluffs are relatively rare because it is difficult to represent the nuts without a diamond, and the bluffer risks running into a random flush card. Since I do not hold a diamond to block the flush and only beat pure air, and considering the large bet size indicates value, I must fold to preserve my chip lead.
